machinery_genes.quote: virB1 , virB2 and virB7 are newly identified, revealing a Rickettsia T4SS lacking only virB5 relative to the vir archetype.
machinery_genes.note: Newly identified Rickettsia rvh component in Gillespie 2009; characterized across 13 Rickettsia genomes (the type-IVA VirB/D4 = rvh system; R. rickettsii is a spotted-fever Rickettsia within this set). Section text: 'VirB1 and related proteins belong to a class of soluble lytic transglycosylases (LTs)'.

machinery_genes.quote: virB1 , virB2 and virB7 are newly identified, revealing a Rickettsia T4SS lacking only virB5 relative to the vir archetype.
machinery_genes.note: Section text: 'VirB2 and related proteins comprise the major pilin subunit of the T pilus and are also essential components of the mating channel'.

machinery_genes.quote: Like many other bacteria harboring T4SSs, Rickettsia contain a VirB3 gene that encodes a small protein (~95 aa) with a predicted signal peptide and a hydrophobic N-terminal region
machinery_genes.note: rvhB3; in most Rickettsia genomes virB4a is clustered with virB3.

machinery_genes.quote: Two VirB4 genes are present in all sequenced Rickettsia genomes ( virB4a and virB4b ), and both encoded proteins are large and similar in size and composition to VirB4 and VirB4-like proteins from other bacteria
machinery_genes.note: Duplicated in rvh (virB4a/virB4b = RvhB4-I/-II); only one copy retains conserved VirB4 features. Counted as one VirB4 family component for the apparatus.

machinery_genes.quote: An extraordinary five copies of the VirB6 gene ( virB6a-e ) are found in most Rickettsia genomes
machinery_genes.note: Tandemly-arrayed five-copy expansion (virB6a-e = rvhB6a-e) unique to rvh; R. massiliae lacks virB6e. Counted as one VirB6 family component.

machinery_genes.quote: virB1 , virB2 and virB7 are newly identified, revealing a Rickettsia T4SS lacking only virB5 relative to the vir archetype.
machinery_genes.note: Section text: 'VirB7 and related proteins are typically the smallest components of T4SS scaffolds'; newly identified in Gillespie 2009.

machinery_genes.quote: Two VirB8 genes are present in Rickettsia genomes ( virB8a and virB8b ), separated by one small ORF (discussed below) and encoded on opposite strands
machinery_genes.note: Duplicated in rvh (virB8a/virB8b = RvhB8-I/-II). Counted as one VirB8 family component.

machinery_genes.quote: Rickettsia genomes contain two VirB9 genes ( virB9a and virB9b ) encoded immediately downstream of the two VirB8 genes
machinery_genes.note: Duplicated in rvh (virB9a/virB9b = RvhB9-I/-II). Counted as one VirB9 family component.

machinery_genes.quote: The Rickettsia VirB10 gene is conserved across 13 genomes (11% div.), and the corresponding amino acid sequence (10.5% div.) is highly conserved

The Rickettsia VirB11 gene is conserved across 13 genomes (9.7% div.) and the corresponding amino acid sequence (4.8% div.) is highly conserved when compared to VirB11 and VirB11-like proteins from other bacteria
machinery_genes.note: rvhB11; single copy. Section header 'Translocation energetics (VirB4 and VirB11)'.

machinery_genes.quote: The Rickettsia VirD4 gene is conserved across 13 genomes (8.1% div.), and the corresponding amino acid sequence (4.2% div.) is highly conserved when compared across divergent pathogenic bacterial type IV coupling proteins (T4CPs)
machinery_genes.note: rvhD4; the T4SS coupling protein (T4CP), single copy. Section header 'Substrate presentation (VirD4)'.

notes: The R. rickettsii Iowa 'VirB' instance is the rickettsial type-IVA T4SS, named rvh (Rickettsiales vir homolog) by Gillespie et al. 2009 (PLoS ONE), the founding characterization paper. That study identified and named every scaffold component across 13 sequenced Rickettsia genomes (spotted-fever and typhus groups, which include R. rickettsii); component naming is transferable to R. rickettsii Iowa as a congeneric member with the conserved rvh locus. Full type-IVA scaffold documented gene-by-gene: VirB1, VirB2, VirB3, VirB4, VirB6, VirB7, VirB8, VirB9, VirB10, VirB11, VirD4. Three families are duplicated in rvh (VirB4a/b, VirB8a/b, VirB9a/b) and VirB6 is a five-copy tandem array (virB6a-e); each is listed once as its Vir family. virB5 is the only VirB/D4 archetype component absent ('lacking only virB5 relative to the vir archetype'), so it is not listed. Deliberately excluded: translocated effectors/substrates (the proximity-rule targets, e.g. RrIowa_0604), chaperones, and regulators. No low-confidence calls; every gene has a verbatim naming quote verified against the PMC HTML of the 2009 paper. The 2010 and 2016 Gillespie reviews are cited for context (rvh nomenclature, RvhB-I/-II paralog naming, scaffold component listing) but all per-gene quotes come from the 2009 founding paper.
